sentences.text: Well, we started early in the morning.
We had to get ready to-- to go out of Plaszow to-- let's say to Emalia to work.
When we would come back in-- in the evening, we were marching five in a row, as I mentioned before, with my grandma, my aunt, my mother, and my-- my cousin.
And very often when we would come back from work, we were called to additional work carrying parts of the barracks that they were building there.
There was always construction going on.
So it didn't matter, men or women, when they needed workers, we were carrying the building parts.
And always we-- I-- I made sure that I am with-- with my mother.
My-- my-- my greatest fear was always to-- of being separated from her.
Came to a point that I don't know whether she was taking care of me or I was taking care of-- of her.
[BACKROUND NOISE] We were extremely close always.
And-- and every time we would come to-- to Plaszow we-- we would find out that there was an action, or somebody was shot, or somebody was hung.
And there were those-- they used to collect us all in the appellplatz for whatever purpose, either to watch the execution or just simply to-- to-- to mistreat us.
[BACKROUND NOISE] And.
Well, food was rationed.
There was so much so of black bread and some soup.

And we were fortunate because when we [BACKROUND NOISE] were going out, let's say to Emalia, this Teckla, who lived on the outskirts of Krakow, was able to come and somehow smuggle in a little bit of some additional food.
And that was always helpful.
And she also was begging us whenever she had a chance in Emalia to talk to us that we should run away, that she'll be able to-- to hide us, and we would try to survive the war this way.
[BACKROUND NOISE] Of course, this was a temptation, but on the other hand, we were afraid of being discovered.
And besides, we were in a group-- or in a line of five of us working to and from Emalia to work.
If one of us was missing, the whole-- all the rest-- was-- the whole five were responsibility for-- for the missing people, and they would be shot.
So that was one of the reason why it never entered even our mind to-- to go with her.
Sporadically, yes.
There-- there-- I remember there were.
Yeah.
Some were and some weren't.
Well, at the beginning, we were-- we wore our own clothes.
And then after awhile, we were [BACKROUND NOISE] given the striped suits made of a very coarse fabric, blue and gray striped.
Women wore-- had dresses and men wear two piece uniforms.
Well, we had what was called washrooms and latrines, which were really outhouses.
Was the one-- the-- the washrooms was one long room with sinks and faucet above it.
And this was a communal thing.
Everybody was washing there.
And then latrines were like outhouses with one long board and several holes in it.
And that was for the physiological needs.
Well, we were-- let's say when it came to washing we managed.
When-- while we were in the factory in Emalia, for instance, we managed to do our washing there.
And there-- it wasn't that much-- we didn't have that much to change.
But somehow whether it was a piece of underwear to wash it and hang it on a-- you need an oven for it to-- to dry fast.
And this is how we maintained our cleanliness.
And I-- I remember when-- let's say when we had to-- were able to wash in the washroom in Plaszow.
And whoever was in charge of turning on the water or turning off the water, they had an order to turn off the water.
So we could have been standing there naked full of soap, and the men came and they have to turn off the water.
And there was nothing we could-- we could do about it.
I even remember a moment that I was-- I didn't even realize that I was naked.
And the-- the men came to turn off the water.
All I did is cover my face.
And I said-- I didn't want him to see me.
And I said, please leave it for a few more minutes.
You know, there was a situation like this that didn't even think of being embarrassed.
I just didn't want him to recognize my face.
That was-- that was about all.
You were a young teenager--
Yes.
Yes, very, very, very lovely childhood with summer vacations on the Baltic Sea.
Or abroad I used to go quite often with my mother.
Winter there was skiing vacations.
It was-- I don't say that everybody led a life like that, but I was fortunate enough.
My father was well to do.
And, well, I knew during the war-- I knew this was the war, and it's not going to last forever.
And I just didn't dwell on the times-- the way it used to be.
That-- that's now and I was happy, fortunate to be with my mother.
And we tried just to-- to-- to survive.
We managed on soups and bread-- rations of bread.
We-- we-- we managed.
And, as I said, when we were in Emalia this Teckla used to once in a while to smuggle someone a little bit of additional food, which-- which always helped.

Well, the news trickled here and there, but nobody could even imagine-- until we got to Auschwitz, I could never imagine-- I could never conceive-- what a-- what a concentration camp or what an extermination camp is like.
Don't forget Plaszow was a labor camp-- forced labor camp.
But we were there with people whom we knew.
And we had a contact with-- with people outside of Plaszow.
So that-- that was bad.
What was horrible was the killings, and hangings, and so on.
But we were sort of on our own-- on our own territory.
Fortunately not.
We were just petrified of him and his two tremendous dogs.
We knew that at his whim he could provoke them and they would just jump at-- without any reason at-- at working people, and bite and kill them at--
Well, while we were going out to-- out of Plaszow to work in-- in Emalia in enamelware factory, there were-- there were Jewish so called [GERMAN] men, the policemen who were escorting us.
And there were also German guards.
But we were going to work and this was like-- almost like a routine.
Except for coming back to Plaszow and finding out what transpired during the day.
How many were killed?
And our actions when the-- when the children were taken away-- I-- I wasn't there.
My husband will tell you more-- more about it because he-- he remembers it very distinctly.
And then at March '43, I think so, the director of the enamelware factory, Schindler, decided to-- to create a sub camp in-- on the premises of Emalia.
And he built barracks and he detained all his workers.
And from then on, we lived there on premises, and we worked there.
So we did not have to go anymore to Plaszow and that was a tremendous relief.
We-- we sort of felt protected by Schindler.
We were not-- not afraid of shootings, and selections, and so on.
We knew we have to work.
We-- we were relatively safe for the time being, that Schindler won't let anything bad happen to us.
Yes.
Well, they were different.
There were two levels, the lower and upper.
And there was a like a communal stove in the-- in the center of the barrack.
And yes, they were-- not a question how different they were, but we had a little bit of peace of mind.
We knew that if Schindler is with us he-- he will protect us.
We somehow trusted the man.
We-- we-- we believed that-- that he will do whatever is in his power to-- to help us, to pull us through.
I mean, one could-- when one had a boyfriend, one had a brother, or a husband, and one would approach Schindler, listen, could-- could you arrange that he could come to the factory and work.
And he-- he-- if he could, he obliged.
It's true.
And when we had some-- I don't mean we-- I, personally, but people had some valuables, diamonds, and we were giving it to Schindler.
But he also needed a lot of valuables to-- to bribe the Germans.
And there was even an incidental where a single girl became pregnant in Emalia, while we were already living there.
And Schindler arranged for a-- an abortion.
And this is unheard of because normally a girl like this would have been shot.
But one could approach Schindler and trust him.
You-- you spoke to him like to-- to-- to a good uncle, you know.
I-- I don't remember any-- I really couldn't say anything-- anything bad about him.
Once we were-- once he create-- once he created the sub-camp, and we were detained in Emalia, yes, we worked-- then he converted the enamelware factory into an ammunition factory.
And while I worked on a machine that was making threads on a certain metal parts was a precision machine.
And this was my-- my job.
Well, of course, I was in a little different situation knowing that my-- my-- my father and my brother are safe, in a way.
And I was always hoping that this will end, and we will be reunited.
But in the meantime, after work, we would always-- we were talking with-- women were in separate barracks and men were in separate barracks.
And you were maybe surprised, but in the evening we were even singing and telling jokes.
We were trying to-- to-- to keep ourselves alive in a relatively good-- I don't know if it's a good mood, but make it bearable for us.
And besides we were not all the time frightened with having Schindler around us.
That was a tremendous comfort.
We were in-- with Schindler on Emalia we were until October 1944.
And then he was moved-- he moved the factory to Brunnlitz in Sudetenland, Czechoslovakia.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the-- he said that his workers are indispensable.
We are all professionals.
He needs us all.
And he sent the men and the women-- the men went through Gross-Rosen and the women went through Auschwitz.
[DRINKS] We were transported to-- from-- from Emalia, we went to Plaszow, and there we were loaded into freight trains, cattle cars, whatever you-- you call it.
I don't even know.
And we went-- that's how we traveled to Auschwitz, to Birkenau.
Not knowing where we are going.
If I can remember, we loaded many, many women into-- we were in a standing position.
Some maybe could sit down.
I couldn't even tell you how long the trip lasted, but the arrival in-- in-- in Auschwitz was pretty gruesome.
This was in-- in October.
And-- what I remember vividly is when we got off the trains was the-- the-- the chimneys with the smoke coming out of them.
And when we were made aware that what this smoke signifies, that these are people-- people being burned.
This-- this made an incredible impression.
I couldn't tell you now.
Probably I did of Auschwitz-- yes.
I mean, before we were transported there?
Probably I did.
Where we came-- yes, I remember when they were-- we had some possessions with us.
And I remember when we were trying, I and other people, holding onto something.
There was already old timers in-- in Auschwitz and one woman was screaming in Yiddish.
She said, crazy people don't-- because we were holding on.
We didn't want to give up our-- whatever we held.
Said, crazy people.
Don't you know where you are?
You won't need anything.
Look at the chimneys.
Look at the smoke.
And-- but there was rumor-- was rumor that Schindler is going to get us out of there.
Anyway, the-- the-- the three weeks in-- in Auschwitz this was a nightmare, a hellish nightmare.
This is beyond description.
And it was October, and this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was cold, windy, and we had those appellplatz and selection standing for hours without any reason.
There were selections because-- and we weren't quite sure.
On one hand, we knew Schindler is going to get us out.
But yet when they were separating people, we didn't know what that meant.
There was a moment that they separated my mother and me.
And I just became frantic.
I-- I thought that I'll never see her again, and I started screaming.
And she managed to-- to work her way back to the-- to the-- to Schindler's group.
And then I knew it-- for the time being, it was safe.
We lived in the barracks with a big-- again, the wooden bunk beds with straw on it.
Oh, I forgot.
When we arrived there we were taken-- they took away all our clothing.
They-- they shaved us.
They shaved and cut our head very-- chopped them very short.
They gave us clothing and after we were-- we were taken to the-- [INAUDIBLE], what do you call it.
This is a bath, and they-- disinfecting bath.
And then the gave us the clothing.
And-- and, for instance, somebody who was size 16 got a dress size 10, and vice versa.
And we looked at each and we burst out laughing.
We looked like a-- like a group from the circus.
It was incredible how ridiculous we all looked.
And the-- the three weeks in Auschwitz consisted mostly, mainly of standing on the appellplatz.
They were taking us out of the barracks.
We were standing there for hours without any rhyme or reason.
Until [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were-- I remember-- walking through the muddy grounds there.
We-- we were-- they took away our shoes and they gave us the Dutch wooden shoes.
And I-- I remember just walking around and thinking to myself, my-- my dad should see me now.
This-- this cannot be-- again, the same thought-- this cannot be the end.
There-- there-- there was always a spark-- a spark of hope.
And sure enough, after three weeks, we found out that we are the so-called [? iron ?] list of Schindler.
And we were transported to-- to Brunnlitz.
Yes.
I remember-- I remember only our group of-- there was 300, or so, women.
And we-- we kept together.
And--
Yes.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
There were guards beating and rushing us.
You know, we were not walking.
We were supposed to go in a sort of jogging rhythm.
And-- and-- and the screams, and the chimneys, and even the old timers, their younger women, they were-- they were sort of wild and beastly.
It was-- it's like a-- like a scene from Hieronymus painting-- Bosch paintings, you know?
It's just incredible to-- it's impossible to describe.
And it's so many years now, but the dread and the fear was-- was horrendous.
I think we were sent to-- to barracks as a-- you know, these were barracks with windows, without paints, without-- I'm not talking about sanitary conditions.
There was a big pail in the center for everybody to relieve themselves.
The routines.
We didn't work there.
We were not working.
The-- the-- our only occupation was being assembled on the appellplatz, the-- and just standing there for hours with the blustery wind blowing.
You know, October in Poland can be rather cold.
And-- And there were selection.
There were selection by the Dr.
Mengele.
Yes, we had the-- the pleasure of seeing him.
All I remember is we tried to make ourselves as invisible as-- as possible.
The women was scraping the red dye from the bricks and putting it on their-- their cheeks to-- to look healthy and--
It-- it was incredible that a man-- decent looking, good looking-- could be so-- well, at that time we knew he had a nickname, the Angel of Death.
That was his nickname in camp.
And we knew that said this was a man to be petrified of.
Yes.
I-- I don't think-- well, there were sending at his whim-- some women-- the selections were often made when we were naked.
So some that were robust and-- and healthy looking were sent to the good side to work.
And those were skinny to the-- was when they are going with the transport.
But that was-- they-- when the selections were made, it wasn't known that we were the untouchable Schindler's list.
So we were just as petrified and-- until it turned out that Schindler arranged for the whole group to come to Brunnlitz.
Well together with us, my grandmother came to Auschwitz.
She was at that time already over 80 years old.
And she was complaining to the SS woman.
And the SS woman hit her.
Anyways, she reported to a [GERMAN], which is a sickly-- a-- a hospital in Auschwitz.
And she was taken there, and we have never seen her since.
She didn't realize, of course, that she shouldn't be in a hospital, that she should stay with us as--
At that time, no, no, no no, no.
You know, there were rumors.
I-- I-- I really don't know exactly how, and we-- we trusted him.
And finally one day after three weeks, all the Schindler women were-- were assembled and put on-- on trains and shipped to-- to Brunnlitz.
Well, of course, we were elated, but in the back of our minds were the thoughts, who knows whether we really are going to Schindler?
You know?
And-- and finally we-- we arrived there.
And the-- the men were waiting for their husbands and wives.
We had no men relatives there because my-- my father and my brother was-- were away.
And then the life-- we started again working.
He had this ammunition factory in-- in Brunnlitz.
The food was rationed.
But we were-- we felt that as long as Schindler is-- is the one who is taking care of us, somehow we were-- and he kept assuring us, don't worry.
You will survived the war.
You know?
So that was a very-- gave-- gave us hope.
Did you have any personal experiences with Mr.
Schindler?
Any need to go to him personally?
Or any--
No, not really.
Don't forget he was-- as much as we trusted him and believed in him, he was still a German.
A good German, and a decent German.
But he was the head director on the-- in the factory, and we were quite in awe of him.
One could approach him and speak to him, but I had no-- no particular reason to-- to speak to him.
If I wanted something then there was this cousin, [?
Bunker, ?] and we always could-- he was the intermediary.
We could always speak to-- to him.
Yes, yeah.
That's true.
No, I was assigned to a machine, and this is what I was doing.
And I-- I don't think that I even realized that he didn't want this-- that he wanted this ammunition-- that he wanted it to be a faulty production.
I don't think I-- I realized it at that time.
I just I was doing my work.
And--
Well, in Brunnlitz we were there from end of October, beginning of November, until the liberation, which was May, 1945.
While we were in Brunnlitz, were-- yes, Schindler did allow people to-- to listen to radio in certain parts of the factory day.
I was not the one, but--
We were still working.
We were factory workers.
I worked night shifts.
My mother worked day shift.
And-- and we were pretty tired in the evening.
And there was no-- this was the normal life.
This was-- working.
And--
Whatever we were-- food ration we were assigned.
And--
Yes.
Of course.
As a matter of fact the military commandant of the-- in Brunnlitz was a-- was a German.
Leipholdt was his name.
And we were quite afraid of him.
And there was a time that he-- he did get an order or-- anywhere, he wanted to liquidate all of us before the-- before the end of the war.
And Schindler managed to get an order for him that he is recalled to the front lines.
In-- in Brunnlitz?
I cannot really recall any.
Maybe [?
Lewie ?] will.
Yeah, I-- I--
